data.title: Charting The U.S. Consumer With Kristofer Kraus
data.description:
Rising home equity has fueled U.S. consumer strength

U.S. consumers have benefited from a steady increase in homeowner equity over the past several decades, which has been a key driver of rising household net worth. As of late 2024, the median net worth of U.S. homeowners is approximately $400,000, while that of renters is about $10,400 – a nearly 40-fold difference in household wealth.1

The growth in homeowners’ net worth is supported by record-high home values, low long-term debt costs (many households secured low fixed-rate mortgages during 2020–2021), and elevated savings and cash reserves.

Combined with a strong labor market and wage growth, these factors contribute to a resilient U.S. consumer base that is well-positioned to navigate economic challenges and tighter lending conditions.

Increasing homeowner equity

Active underwriting reveals risks concealed by inflated credit scores

However, while U.S. household balance sheets remain broadly healthy, delinquencies are rising. During the pandemic, widespread fiscal stimulus, loan forbearance, and suppression of delinquent collections led to an artificial boost in credit scores, a phenomenon we call “FICO inflation.”

Average FICO scores surged, peaking around 715 in 2021. Today, some of these borrowers are delinquent or even defaulting: As the pandemic-related supports rolled off, credit performance normalized, exposing vulnerabilities among borrowers whose scores were temporarily elevated.

PIMCO Equifax panel average vantage score

Lower-tier borrowers showed greater FICO inflation, with higher-tier borrowers relatively unaffected

In particular, lower-tier (601–650) and mid-tier (661–720) credit bands saw the biggest lift, with FICO scores jumping 25–30 points and 20–25 points, respectively.

By contrast, top-tier borrowers (781–850) saw little to no impact. Recalibrations to the score-calculating model may have amplified the net effect.

Overall, FICO inflation appears to have given a disproportionate – and in many cases temporary – benefit to riskier borrowers.

Average credit score impact across credit buckets

Lending connected to resilient U.S. homeowners is increasingly attractive in a divergent market

Delinquencies are increasingly bifurcated by consumer asset class.

Some borrowers are faring well: As of Q4 2024, serious delinquencies (90+ days) in mortgages, home equity line of credit, and student loans remain near historical lows, supported by home equity gains, fixed-rate debt structures, and policy tailwinds.

In contrast, credit cards and auto loans – particularly among lower-tier borrowers – have seen a steady rise in delinquencies since 2022, reflecting inflation pressures and depleted pandemic-era savings. Student loans may come under greater pressure in the months ahead as pandemic-related forbearance programs end.

Thoughtful analysis of consumer loan pools is critical to managing the risk/reward balance in asset-based finance.

Delinquencies across consumer loan types

Tighter underwriting standards bolster resilience in consumer lending

Facing these delinquency trends, personal loan originators have become more cautious in 2024 and 2025, underwriting to some of the tightest credit standards in recent history. Average FICO scores on new originations now approach 730, up from below 700 just three years ago, while borrower income has climbed to $140,000 on average – more than a 25% increase since 2022.

These shifts reflect careful credit analysis and a focus on more reliable borrowers in what’s likely the later stage of the credit cycle.

The result of stricter underwriting standards is a more defensive and resilient personal loan market, with structural tailwinds for performance.

Originations by income and by FICO

Footnote

1 According to the Aspen Institute

Disclosures

Past performance is not a guarantee or a reliable indicator of future results.

Investing in the bond market is subject to risks, including market, interest rate, issuer, credit, inflation risk, and liquidity risk. The value of most bonds and bond strategies are impacted by changes in interest rates. Bonds and bond strategies with longer durations tend to be more sensitive and volatile than those with shorter durations; bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise, and low interest rate environments increase this risk. Reductions in bond counterparty capacity may contribute to decreased market liquidity and increased price volatility. Bond investments may be worth more or less than the original cost when redeemed.

Statements concerning financial market trends or portfolio strategies are based on current market conditions, which will fluctuate. There is no guarantee that these investment strategies will work under all market conditions or are appropriate for all investors and each investor should evaluate their ability to invest for the long term, especially during periods of downturn in the market. Outlook and strategies are subject to change without notice.

Performance results for certain charts and graphs may be limited by date ranges specified on those charts and graphs; different time periods may produce different results.

It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index.

PIMCO as a general matter provides services to qualified institutions, financial intermediaries and institutional investors. Individual investors should contact their own financial professional to determine the most appropriate investment options for their financial situation. This material contains the opinions of the manager, and such opinions are subject to change without notice. This material has been distributed for informational purposes only and should not be considered as investment advice or a recommendation of any particular security, strategy or investment product. Information contained herein has been obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but not guaranteed. No part of this material may be reproduced in any form, or referred to in any other publication, without express written permission. PIMCO is a trademark of Allianz Asset Management of America LLC in the United States and throughout the world.

CMR2025-0604-4552073

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 06/07/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: S&T Bancorp: Earnings Likely To Decline, Stock Appears Fairly Valued
data.description:
Earnings of S&T Bancorp, Inc. (NASDAQ:STBA) will likely come under pressure this year from higher provisions expense for loan losses. Further, subdued loan growth will most probably get canceled out by net interest margin compression. I’m expecting the company’s earnings to drop by 9% this year to $3.11 per share. As I’ll discuss in greater detail below, the stock appears fairly valued with a moderate dividend yield. Hence, I’m adopting a hold rating on S&T Bancorp.

Loan Pipelines are Robust, but Loan Growth will Ultimately Depend on Macros

S&T Bancorp’s loan portfolio grew by 1.3% in the first quarter of 2025, or 5.0% annualized, which is a big improvement from last year when the annual loan growth was only 1.3%. Not only was the company able to boost its outstanding loan balance, but it also substantially increased loans in the pipeline. The management mentioned in the conference call that pipelines were up nearly 40% since the end of 2024, primarily in the commercial and consumer segments. Although the pipeline for home equity lines of credit (“HELOC”) also grew, but the residential mortgage pipeline declined, which balanced each other out.

As mentioned in the conference call, the recent hiring of new bankers drove the pipeline growth. S&T Bancorp is in the process of hiring even more new bankers in its Commercial and Industrial (“C&I”) division, which should further enhance the pipeline.

However, pipeline growth does not necessarily mean that the loan balance will also increase this year. Prospective borrowers, especially in the commercial real estate segment, may delay their projects as they adopt a wait-and-see approach in the wake of the tariff uncertainty. The U.S. is yet to strike deals with its biggest trade partners, including China, Canada, and Mexico. As per the latest news, the leaders of the U.S. and China had a telephone call to discuss trade and China’s rare-earth metals. Nevertheless, I think any deal could be months in the making, if not years.

Meanwhile, the tariffs have already started to take a toll on the US economy. Firstly, applications for U.S. unemployment benefits last week rose to the highest level since October 2024, as per news reports. Further, a poll of business executives by the Association of International Certified Professional Accountants shows that only 27% of the respondents are now confident about the economic outlook, down from 47% of respondents in 1Q-2025 and 67% in 4Q-2024. Moreover, the OECD has recently reduced its GDP growth estimate for the U.S. to 1.6% from the previous estimate of 2.2%, as per news reports.

Keeping in mind the pipelines and the macroeconomic trends, I’m expecting the loan portfolio to grow by 2.8% in 2025.

Margin to Resume Downtrend as Rates Fall

After declining in the latter part of 2025, S&T Bancorp’s net interest margin rose by 4 basis points in the first quarter of 2025. However, I think the first quarter’s performance will likely turn out to be a blip in the otherwise downward trend. I’m expecting the net interest margin to resume its downtrend in the second half of 2025.

I’m expecting the Fed to cut rates by 50 basis points in the second half of this year. The possibility of an economic recession will drive the rate cut. But the Fed will most probably not cut rates too much because the tariffs are likely to push up inflation soon. The effect of tariffs is not yet visible on inflation numbers, but I’m expecting it to become visible soon. This is because Walmart, the biggest retailer in the U.S., announced in May that it was going to raise its prices, according to news reports.

In the past, S&T Bancorp’s net interest margin has loosely followed the trend of the Fed funds rate, as shown below.

The results of the management’s rate-sensitivity simulation model given in the 10-Q filing also show that a rate cut could have a negative impact on the net interest income for two years after the cut.

Therefore, I’m expecting the net interest margin to remain almost stable in the second quarter of this year and then drop by 4 basis points in the second half of 2025.

Provision Reversals to End Soon

S&T Bancorp has reversed its previous provisioning (or allowance) for expected loan losses for three consecutive quarters now. The reversals can naturally not continue for long because the allowance-to-loan ratio has fallen to 1.26%, the lowest level since 2019. There is not much allowance left to reverse.

Moreover, the threats from the trade policy uncertainty will likely encourage the management to boost its reserves for loan losses. As I discussed above, the effects of the tariffs have already started to become visible on the job market. Therefore, I’m expecting the recent trend of provision reversals to end in the second quarter. I’m expecting the provision expense to rise a bit to $6 million for the last three quarters of the year, leading to a full-year provision expense of $3 million.

Expecting the EPS to Decline by ~9%

To summarize the above discussion, the loan portfolio will likely grow this year, which will support the bottom line. However, the net interest margin will likely decline, which will cancel out the benefit of the loan growth such that the net interest income will remain almost unchanged from last year’s level.

Further, I’m expecting the provision expense to rise in the year ahead, which will hurt earnings. To arrive at my EPS estimate for this year, I’m making the following assumptions.

  1. To forecast the fee income (part of non-interest income), I’m taking the management’s guidance given in the conference call.
  2. I’m expecting the securities loss (also a part of non-interest income) to rise in the second quarter because of the ongoing bond market turbulence. Yields at the long end of the treasury yield curve have jumped up, thereby reducing the market value of long-tenor bonds.
  3. I’m using the management’s guidance of the expense run rate to forecast the non-interest expenses.

Overall, I’m expecting earnings to decline by 9% year-over-year to $3.11 per share in 2025.

Adopting a Hold Rating

I last wrote a report on S&T Bancorp back in April 2023. At that time, I adopted a buy rating on the stock with a December 2023 target price of $37.4. Following the release of my report, STBA’s stock price rose from $26.79 (at the time of the report publication) to $33.42 at the end of December 2023, showing a massive growth of 25%. Therefore, I’m pleased with my buy call, even if the stock price didn’t reach my price target.

To determine the new price target for December-end 2025, I’m using the historical average price-to-earnings (“P/E”) and price-to-tangible book (“P/TB”) multiples given below.

Multiplying the average P/E ratio with my EPS forecast gives a target price of $32.6 for the end of 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB ratio with my TBVPS forecast gives a target price of $41.2 for the end of 2025.

The average price target comes out to $36.9, which is 0.7% below the closing market price for June 6, 2025.

S&T Bancorp also offers a moderate dividend yield of 3.7% at a quarterly dividend of $0.34 per share. My earnings estimate and the current dividend payout suggest a payout ratio of 44% for 2025, which is easily sustainable even if it’s slightly higher than the last four-year average of 37%. Therefore, I think the dividend payout is safe.

Adding the dividend yield to the price downside gives a total expected return of just 2.9%. Therefore, I’m adopting a hold rating on S&T Bancorp this time. The company’s earnings will most probably get pressured this year by a rise in the provision expense and a fall in the net interest margin.

data.title: Beyond Meat: Significant Deterioration In Results During Early 2025
data.description:
Beyond Meat's (NASDAQ:BYND) Q1 2025 results were quite poor, with net revenues ending up down -9% year-over-year compared to expectations for roughly flat growth. The weak sales also drove Beyond Meat's gross margins down and resulted in it generating negative $42 million in adjusted EBITDA during the quarter.

Beyond Meat did shore up its liquidity with a $100 million secured delayed-draw term loan. This pushes common shareholders further down in the capital structure though and also weakens the position of the convertible noteholders.

I believe that the intrinsic value of Beyond Meat's common stock is zero, although current shareholders could still be left with a token amount of value if Beyond Meat avoids an in-court restructuring. The total value of the business is declining as Beyond Meat struggles to meet modest expectations, though.

Q1 2025 Results

Beyond Meat's Q1 2025 results ended up significantly worse than expected. It had provided guidance for its Q1 2025 net revenues to be comparable to its Q1 2024 net revenues.

Instead, Beyond Meat's Q1 2025 net revenues ended up at $68.7 million, which was a -9% decrease compared to Q1 2024. Beyond Meat sold 11% fewer pounds in Q1 2025 compared to Q1 2024, slightly offsetting this with a 2% increase in net revenue per pound.

Beyond Meat's US retail and foodservice channels (combined accounting for around 60% of its revenues) particularly struggled, with total pounds sold down -23% year-over-year. Beyond Meat attributed this to economic uncertainty in the US and other macroeconomic trends.

The lower volumes weighed negatively on Beyond Meat's gross margins, which were reported at -1.5% for Q1 2025. This gross margin number did include some non-cash changes to increase inventory provision, as well as some expenses related to its decision to suspend operational activities in China. Excluding those items, Beyond Meat's gross margin would have been approximately 6% in Q1 2025.

Beyond Meat reported negative $42 million in adjusted EBITDA for Q1 2025 and saw its cash position decline from $146 million at the end of 2024 to $116 million at the end of Q1 2025.

Future Outlook

Due to its weak Q1 2025 results and what it calls "an elevated level of uncertainty within its operating environment", Beyond Meat has withdrawn its full-year guidance and now expects $80 million to $85 million in net revenues in Q2 2025. This would be a -9% to a -14% decrease compared to Q2 2024.

Beyond Meat now seems likely to end up with under $300 million in net revenues for 2025, much lower than its original guidance for $320 million to $335 million.

Beyond Meat's gross margins are also likely to fall well short of the 20% it originally expected for 2025. It does expect improved gross margins as it increases production at its Pennsylvania production facility (having consolidated its production network). However, I'd more realistically expect gross margins to potentially improve to the mid-teens in the second half of the year.

Thus, I am now expecting Beyond Meat to have over $100 million in cash burn during 2025. It still is targeting reaching positive EBITDA (on a run rate basis) by the end of 2026, but that's a long way off.

Cash Burn And Liquidity

With over $100 million in cash burn, Beyond Meat's cash position would fall under $46 million by the end of 2025.

Beyond Meat has shored up its liquidity with a secured delayed-draw term loan of $100 million that it arranged with Unprocessed Foods, which is a subsidiary of the Ahimsa Foundation. The Ahimsa Foundation is a Colorado nonprofit that advocates for animal welfare and veganism. It has been connected with other investments in the plant-based food sector before.

This term loan has initial maturity of February 2030 and an interest rate of 12% (payable in kind) on any drawings. Beyond Meat may draw on its term loan facility until February 2026.

Unprocessed Foods is also getting approximately 9.6 million warrants with an exercise price between $2.00 and $3.75 (calculated at 115% of Beyond Meat's daily volume weighted average price over the 30-day period starting on May 8).

There is also a provision in the term loan agreement that caps the amount of cash that Beyond Meat can use to repay (at maturity) its convertible notes due 2027 at $60 million, plus the cash proceeds from any equity offerings.

Notes On Valuation

The addition of secured debt (when drawn) at the top of its capital structure weakens the position of the convertible noteholders. If Beyond Meat draws $100 million from its delayed-draw term loan near the end of 2025, it may have around $115 million outstanding on that term loan by the time the convertible notes mature in March 2027.

If there ends up being an in-court restructuring, the convertible noteholders may receive very little return (if anything), particularly if Beyond Meat falls short of its goal of reaching positive adjusted EBITDA.

Thus the convertible noteholders may be willing to settle for a modest amount of cash (capped by the term loan agreement as noted above) plus warrants/shares.

Beyond Meat's common stock has an intrinsic value of $0 due to all of the debt ahead of it in the capital structure. However, common shareholders could still be left with a token amount of value depending on how the debt negotiations play out.

Conclusion

Beyond Meat's Q1 2025 results were pretty dismal as demand for its products faltered amidst an uncertain macro environment. It reported a -9% decline in net revenues for the quarter and expects Q2 2025 results to show a similar or slightly worse decline.

Beyond Meat did arrange for a $100 million secured delayed-draw term loan, which should give it enough liquidity to get through 2026. Between Beyond Meat's poor results and the 12% PIK interest on its term loan, there may not be much value left over beyond its secured debt, though.

data.title: Jobs Growth Is Moderate But Likely To Slow
data.description:
A quick post focusing on today's May '25 jobs report.

Anyone who has followed the monthly jobs numbers for a few years knows that they are volatile and subject to significant revisions from time to time. I've tried to correct for this by stepping back and trying to see the long-term trends and whether they are changing.

As this chart shows, private sector jobs (the ones that really count) have been growing at an annual rate of a bit more than 1% for most of the past 18 months. Over the past decade, private sector jobs have grown at an annualized rate of 1.3%, while real GDP growth has been about 2.3% per year.

So what we are seeing today is roughly par for the course; nothing to get excited or worried about. Except that the number of immigrants entering the labor market is in the process of slowing rather dramatically, thanks to Trump's closed borders and aggressive efforts to deport illegals.

My back of the envelope calculation says that without any meaningful policy changes, jobs growth is going to slow to somewhat less than 1% a year - possibly to as low as 0.6% a year by the end of this year. This is going to leave us with an economy that struggles to grow 2% a year.

This is not the stuff of booms. For a booming economy, we need to see significant reductions in tax and regulatory burdens. Fortunately, this is something that Trump is working hard to achieve, so there is hope for a better economy in the years to come.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: HKND: Principles Taking Priority Over Performance
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Unlike other socially responsible-themed funds, the Humankind US Stock ETF (NYSEARCA:HKND) doesn't try to fleece investors by charging exuberant fees for the privilege of investing responsibly. However, by the firm's own admission, the strategy of selecting stocks based on their economic impact on humankind is "untested and unproven," and a review of its fundamentals and performance indicates HKND is inferior to other large-cap funds on the market. As such, I don't trust it to deliver strong returns moving forward, so I've decided to assign a "sell" rating to this ETF despite its admirable objective.

HKND Overview

Strategy Discussion and Peer Group

HKND tracks the Humankind US Equity Index, which, according to the fund's website, "is designed to make it easy for you to invest in the manner that is best for you and humanity." The fund's prospectus adds that the strategy "is consistent with the Company's purpose of creating socially optimal economic outcomes through investing in issuers that provide investor value, consumer value, employee value, and societal value in order to benefit humankind." The methodology document describes each of these value propositions as follows:

1. Investor Value: the value a company is estimated to provide to its investors on the basis of multi-year profitability.

2. Consumer Value: the value a company is estimated to provide to its customers based on its offerings of products and services.

3. Employee Value: the value a company is estimated to provide to its employees based on their salaries, bonuses, and benefits.

4. Societal Value: each company receives a number of debits and credits reflecting the value of the estimated external unaccounted costs or benefits to society from operating its business.

The firm's 2024 Human Benefit Report describes how the selection process also assigns scores, both positive and negative, based on factors like food production and distribution, tobacco, shelter, predatory lending, and water pollution. However, it's crucial that readers know that the strategy is "untested and unproven," as noted in the ETF's own investment case. As written:

While we believe companies that treat people well will be more successful in the long term for the reasons identified in this document, our investment views are untested and unproven; therefore, there is no guarantee they will come to fruition, generate profits, avoid losses, or generally produce desired results.

It's an honest admission that the ETF is experimental. I didn't see any mention of the use of fundamental analysis in the firm's literature beyond the "Investor Value" component, but at least HKND's fees aren't prohibitive. I've covered many ETFs that try to convince investors to align their values with their investments and then go ahead and charge outrageously high annual fees. However, HKND's expense ratio is just 0.11% and is competitive with the funds it hopes to outperform, four of which I've listed below.

The SPDR S&P 1500 Composite Stock Market ETF (SPTM) represents HKND's official benchmark. However, third-party websites like Morningstar and my own fundamental analysis strongly suggest HKND functions more like a large-cap value fund, which is why I'll also benchmark it against the SPDR S&P 1500 Value Tilt ETF (VLU) and the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD). Lastly, I want to throw the Harbor Human Capital Factor US Large Cap ETF (HAPI) into the mix. It is somewhat similar to HKND in that it prefers companies that treat its employees well, and its fundamentals were strong enough to earn a "buy" rating from me in September.

Performance Snapshot

HKND launched a little over four years ago on February 23, 2021. Since then, its results have been disappointing, delivering just a 35.53% total return compared to 59.34% and 51.66% for SPTM and VLU, the two S&P 1500 Composite Index benchmarks. IWD gained about 5% more than HKND (40.57% vs. 35.53%), but unfortunately, HAPI only launched on October 12, 2022, so it couldn't be used in this performance comparison (though it has about a 50% lead over HKND since its inception).

HKND has been the least volatile of the four ETFs noted above, evidenced by its 14.21% annualized standard deviation since March 2021, per Portfolio Visualizer. However, that hasn't translated to better drawdowns than VLU and IWD, and it's clearly lagged in rising markets. For example, its 10.67% gain in 2024 was the lowest by almost 4%, and its worst year figure of -9.14% also suggests that you'd be better off from an investment perspective with the more straightforward VLU and IWD.

HKND Analysis

Sector Allocations: Heavy on Health Care

A key reason for HKND's value-lean is that it consistently overweights Health Care stocks. Today, that allocation is above 40% and is responsible for the fund's poor 5.99/10 Sector Diversification Score, an internal metric of mine that compares an ETF's sector composition against a perfectly diversified and perfectly undiversified portfolio. You'll see SPTM, VLU, IWD, and even HAPI are better diversified.

HKND Fundamental Analysis

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for HKND's top 25 holdings, which comprise 42.92% of the portfolio. The Index has a 4% maximum weighting per security at each rebalancing, and it appears as though Alphabet (GOOGL) is the only one to reach that threshold, implying it has the best "humankind" score on the market. Other top stocks include Apple (AAPL) and Microsoft (MSFT), while Meta Platforms (META) and Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) are missing entirely from the 857-stock fund.

HKND has a relatively low 15.01x forward P/E and 12.03x trailing P/CF, placing it on par with VLU as the fund with the best value proposition. Health Care selections like AbbVie (ABBV), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), and Pfizer (PFE) contribute to the fund's cheap valuations, and they also happen to be less volatile than the market, evidenced by their low five-year betas. Overall, I think these are HKND's best features, and I can see the ETF performing well in defensive markets.

Still, we saw earlier how VLU and IWD can do just as well in such conditions, and since they're better diversified, there's less underperformance risk should Health Care stocks continue to perform poorly. It's worth noting that the Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLV) gained only 5.27% for the three years ending May 2025, ranking #9/11 among the 11 S&P 500 sector ETFs offered by State Street, so momentum is clearly not on HKND's side.

The table also highlights HKND's 19.14% weighted average return on equity, which, compared to VLU and IWD, is pretty solid. However, its 11.29% net margins are 3-4% worse, and the main reason is HKND's high use of leverage. I've bolded its components' 1.56 debt-equity ratio below, which is 0.37 points more than the peer group average and caused primarily by Health Care stocks like AbbVie and Amgen (AMGN). We also see that HKND's margins are below VLU's nearly across the board (the exception is gross margins), and since I put quality first for the long term, this is a disappointing find.

Investment Recommendation

HKND requires investors to buy into a proprietary, untested, and unproven strategy. I appreciate its low 0.11% ER, and even with $151 million in AUM, it's not a hugely profitable product for its creators. However, the opportunity cost of foregoing fundamental analysis can be significant, and my evaluation didn't turn up anything special compared to large-cap value funds like VLU and IWD. Therefore, while I appreciate the fund's objective, I've decided to assign it a "sell" rating. Thank you for reading.

data.title: BDC Weekly Review: Sector ROE Is Too Low, Says Sixth Street Specialty Lending
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company [BDC] sector from both the bottom-up–highlighting individual news and events – as well as the top-down – providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the last week of May.

Market Action

BDCs had another good week with a 2% average return. Over May, the average total return was 5%.

May was the best month for the sector since late 2023 though it doesn't come close to offsetting the drop over the preceding two months.

The sector has continued to rally off its recent trough, if not quite in a straight line.

The median BDC valuation in our coverage is trading at an 8% discount relative to the historic average, which remains attractive for starter positions.

Market Themes

Sixth Street Specialty Lending (TSLX) came out with its investor letter. These are always interesting reading for investors in the BDC sector. One of the messages that was prominent is that the sector ROE either today or going forward is very unattractive. This is based on a number of figures such as the 1% annual credit loss rate, 1.5% base management fee, a base rate of 3.5% for the forward analysis and the lending spread of 5.4% or forward portfolio yield of 8.9%.

This results in BDC returns of 6.7% and 5.2% on a current and going-forward basis. TSLX then contrasts these poor numbers to their own portfolio which boasts a much higher lending spread of 7.5%, additional income via fees and a lower credit loss rate of 0.5%, resulting in returns of 11.8% and 10.3% on a current and forward basis.

This is a useful exercise, however it’s obviously one intended to favor TSLX, which has been a higher-performing BDC. As many investors know, it doesn’t make a lot of sense approaching the BDC sector on an average basis. The dispersion of performance along with serial correlation of performance means that averages don’t present a good picture of the sector.

On an average basis, for example, the return in Q1 was an annualized 5.6% while on a median basis, it was 8% and on the basis of current holdings in our Income Portfolios it was 9.8%. Taking the TSLX assumptions in turn, the average base management fee might very well be 1.5% however only one of our own holdings has the fee at that level or higher.

The spread assumption only makes sense in the context of larger BDCs that operate in the upper middle-market. Spreads in the lower middle-market are significantly higher - FDUS has a portfolio yield of 13.2% for example and the average portfolio yield in our coverage today is 11.5% not 10.2% per TSLX assumption.

The credit loss could be 1% per annum or even higher. However, it is concentrated in the left tail of the sector - it’s not quite the 80/20 rule with 20% of BDCs responsible for 80% of the losses, but it’s not miles off. Four, BDCs in coverage had a total NAV return of -5% on average with the rest of the space having a total NAV return of +2.4% with the average being 1.4%, meaning, the average is not a good guide for what's happening in the sector, particularly as underperformers remain fairly consistent.

In short, sure, the average may not be great, but no one is telling BDC investors to settle for average.

Perhaps the most important thing that TSLX doesn’t talk about is valuation. Yes, the TSLX return is higher, however investors don’t have access to this return (except perhaps the managers who get TSLX shares as compensation). A dollar invested in TSLX only gets 75 cents of NAV performance because of the stock’s huge premium.

A dollar invested in the median BDC in our coverage gets an additional 8% of the return for free. The valuation difference of 45% (8% discount on a median BDC in coverage vs. 37% TSLX premium) makes a big difference on the relative valuation of returns. In fact, if we look at the world on a valuation-adjusted basis, TSLX has a below-median 3-year total NAV return. All in all, the ROE figures are a good exercise to carry out, but as it's presented it's skewed to favor TSLX.

Market Commentary

Ares Capital (ARCC) issued a 2030 5.5% bond. The press release says the funds will be used to repay certain indebtedness under its debt facilities. The company pays around 6.2% on its facilities, so the bond will help to reduce the overall interest expense for the time being. However, the company also has a big maturity coming up - its largest with the 1.25bn of 3.25% July-2025 bonds, so this issuance looks like a partial refinancing of that bond.

In fact, if it simply lets the bond mature, the company’s leverage would drop considerably to 0.93x from 1x. This is not out of the question given the low level of M&A however that would be very low by the company's historic standards. If, as is more likely, the 2030 bond is used to refinance the maturing 2025 bond, it will push the company’s interest expense higher.

The company’s interest expense has been pretty flat for about a year despite the Fed pushing short-term rates lower by 1% which has to do with continued refinancing of its low-coupon maturing bonds to higher coupons. This has been squeezing NII and the recent rise in longer-term rates will continue to squeeze it.

Stance and Takeaways

BDCs continued to grind tighter, with many stocks now fully recovering their Liberation Day drops. Although we don't agree with TSLX that the rest of the sector ROE levels are too low to allocate to, we have partly monetized the gains in our Portfolios from allocations made in the wake of the volatility in the second week of April. Most recently, we reduced our OBDC allocation, which looks to be fully valued now. Volatility is not over in the sector, and we expect better entry points in the coming months.

data.title: Jobs Report Generates 1% Enthusiasm
data.description:
Friday’s market pulled off a tidy hat trick: decent jobs data, a thaw in the Trump-Musk cold war, and China prying open the rare-earth pantry just enough to feign generosity. None of it tilted the field, but together, it prodded bulls to a 1% gain.

Level Change 6/6/25 (%)

+1.1 Dow

+1.2 Nasdaq

+1.0 Nasdaq 100

+1.0 S&P 500

+1.0 S&P 400

+1.2 S&P 600

Stocks rallied across the board, pushing the S&P 500 over the 6,000 mark, more than 20% above its early April low and within 2.5% of February’s record close at 6,144. The world may pull through this trade war after all.

Start with the jobs report.

May nonfarm payrolls rose 139K, beating the 130K consensus, though downward revisions to March and April took a little shine off. The unemployment rate held at 4.2%, supported by a dip in participation - fewer people looking for work. Wages came in hot at +0.4% month-over-month, reigniting the Fed’s internal debate between “wait” and “wait longer.”

In other words, soft landing enthusiasts got their Goldilocks moment.

Employment is holding up, wage growth isn’t collapsing, and there’s just enough uncertainty to keep the Fed motionless. True, no imminent rate cut, but with the effective fed funds rate at 4.3% - lower than it was during the 1980s boom and the dot-com froth of the late ’90s - nobody’s exactly gasping for liquidity.

Tesla (TSLA +3.7%) bounced back from Thursday’s stomach-churning plunge - its second-worst day since 2020 - after reports of a Trump-Musk detente. The White House denied any upcoming phone call but leaked that Trump plans to sell his Tesla, presumably while grousing that it won’t take commands in all caps. The bromance is broken, but for now, the revenge tweets are still in drafts.

Trade got a whiff of progress, too. The White House confirmed high-level US-China talks are set for Monday in London, confirming that this week’s call successfully produced the one thing both sides can always deliver: another meeting.

More important, Beijing cracked open the rare-earth vault ever so slightly, granting export licenses for magnets used in everything from EVs to missiles. The pace remains glacial, and the message clear: these aren’t concessions, they’re bargaining chips. China’s 90% stranglehold on rare-earth magnets makes OPEC look quaint, and they’re wielding it like a Bond villain petting a cat.

One Chinese policy advisor put it plainly: rare earths are leverage, and Beijing intends to use them. It seems to be working. Mercedes-Benz (OTCPK:MBGAF), among others, is already scrambling for supply workarounds that probably don’t exist. If you’re wondering whether geopolitics matters to markets, ask any factory engineer currently Googling “non-magnetic EV propulsion.”

Back in earnings land, Broadcom (AVGO -5%) beat slightly and kept the AI dream alive, but not enough to support high expectations. AI revenue jumped 46% to $4.4B, with CEO Hock Tan promising ten consecutive quarters of growth on hyperscale partners writing blank checks. But the stock still dropped 5% as investors focused less on the gold rush and more on the company’s non-AI business, which still moves at the speed of 2019.

Lululemon (LULU -19.8%) got pulled taut on the tariff rack, cutting its full-year outlook as rising costs and softer demand stretched those yoga pants uncomfortably thin. CFO Meghan Frank broke the news with athleisure-grade calm: gross margins are now expected to drop 110 basis points for the year, with nearly half the drop blamed on tariffs - 30% on China, 10% everywhere else they source. Investors responded like consumers confronting a $98 tank top: they backed away.

The takeaway from Friday and indeed the week is that spring’s tariff-induced anxiety attack is over. The weirdness of this trade war will get ironed out. The craziest threats won’t stick, and companies will forge ahead, likely in moisture-wicking, duty-not-free performance fabric.

How long this optimism holds is Wall Street’s six-thousand-dollar question.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Employment Report: 139K Jobs Added In May, More Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The latest employment report showed that 139,000 jobs were added in May, down from 147,000 in April but higher than the expected 126,000 addition. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate remained at 4.2%, as expected.

Here is an excerpt from the Employment Situation Summary released this morning by the Bureau of Labor Statistics:

Total nonfarm payroll employment increased by 139,000 in May, and the unemployment rate was unchanged at 4.2 percent, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported today. Employment continued to trend up in health care, leisure and hospitality, and social assistance. Federal government continued to lose jobs.

Household Survey Data: The unemployment rate held at 4.2 percent in May and has remained in a narrow range of 4.0 percent to 4.2 percent since May 2024. The number of unemployed people, at 7.2 million, changed little over the month.

Establishment Survey Data: Total nonfarm payroll employment increased by 139,000 in May, similar to the average monthly gain of 149,000 over the prior 12 months. In May, employment continued to trend up in health care, leisure and hospitality, and social assistance. Federal government continued to lose jobs.

Here is a snapshot of the monthly change in nonfarm employment over the last four years. The 3-month moving average is currently at 135,000, up from the previous month.

For another view, here is the monthly percent change in nonfarm employment since 2000. We've added a 12-month moving average to highlight the long-term trend. The latest 12-month moving average is at 144,000, the lowest level in over four years.

Unemployment, Recessions, and Market Trends

The next chart illustrates the relationship between unemployment, recessions, and the S&P Composite since 1948. Unemployment is typically a lagging indicator that moves inversely to equity prices (the top series in the chart).

Notice the rising unemployment peaks in 1971, 1975, and 1982, which coincided with bear markets. A similar pattern briefly emerged during the COVID pandemic, but the impact was short-lived, as irrational exuberance quickly took over.

The latest unemployment rate stands at 4.244% (to three decimal places).

Now, let's examine the unemployment rate as a recession indicator - specifically, the cyclical troughs in the unemployment rate (UR). The next chart highlights a 12-month moving average of the UR, with its troughs marked.

Currently, the unemployment rate stands at 4.24%, slightly above the latest 12-month moving average of 4.15%. As shown in the inset table, the correlation between these moving average troughs and the start of recessions is remarkably strong. The most recent trough occurred 22 months ago, in June 2023, when the 12-month moving average of the unemployment rate fell to its lowest level since January 1970.

The next chart highlights the unemployment rate for those unemployed for 27 weeks or more. This rate has declined significantly from its all-time peak of 4.4% in April 2010. Following the COVID pandemic, it rose as high as 2.6% but has since trended downward. It now stands at 0.9%, down from the previous month.

How long does unemployment typically last? As the next chart shows, the latest data indicates that the average duration of unemployment is 21.8 weeks. Historically, this metric tends to rise during and after recessions.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics’ broadest measure of unemployment, the U6 series, captures not only the unemployed but also the underemployed, marginally attached workers, and those who have stopped looking for work.

Often referred to as the 'real' unemployment rate, many economists consider it the most comprehensive gauge of labor market conditions. In contrast, the more widely cited unemployment rate only includes those who are unemployed and actively job-seeking within the past four weeks.

The U6 series currently stands at 7.8%, unchanged from the previous month.

Labor Market Participation and Structural Shifts

For a broader perspective on the structural shifts in the U.S. labor market, the next chart illustrates the labor force participation rate. This metric measures the percentage of the civilian population aged 16 and over who are either employed or actively seeking work.

It provides a key estimate of an economy’s active workforce and serves as an important indicator of labor market health. Currently, the rate stands at 62.4%, down from the previous month.

Here’s another chart that provides a different perspective on the unemployment rate - unemployment divided by the labor force participation rate. This metric currently stands at 6.8%, up from the previous month.

The next chart overlays the unemployment rate with the employment-population ratio, which measures the number of employed people as a percentage of the total civilian population aged 16 and over. The inverse correlation between the two series is clear.

The data also highlights the accelerating growth of women in the workforce and the rise of two-income households in the early 1980s. Before the COVID pandemic, the employment-population ratio fluctuated between 54.9% and 64.7%.

The lower end of this range dates back to the Eisenhower era - a time of predominantly one-income households, the Korean War, and the novelty of soft drinks being sold in cans. In April 2020, the global pandemic drove the employment-population ratio to a record low of 51.2%. Since then, it has been gradually recovering toward its pre-pandemic range, with the latest reading at 59.7%, the lowest level since January 2022.

Looking ahead, both the employment-population ratio and labor force participation rate will be key metrics to watch. The first wave of baby boomers continues to exert downward pressure on these figures. The oldest boomers became eligible for early retirement at the onset of the Great Recession, and their transition to full retirement age will continue through 2030.

Notes: The start date of 1948 in the charts above was determined by the earliest monthly employment data collected by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. The best source for the historical data is the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis.

The S&P Composite is a splice of the S&P 500, which started in 1957, with the S&P 90, which preceded it. ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), and SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: D.R. Horton: This Homebuilder Has The Potential To Build Value
data.description:
If you're going to be an investor as opposed to a speculator, you better be ready for the volatility that comes with owning shares for an extended window of time. Even though I don't own shares in it, one example that I could point to involves a massive home builder by the name of D.R. Horton (NYSE:DHI). Back in December of last year, I decided to reaffirm the "buy" rating that I had on the stock. This was based on how cheap shares were and was in spite of the fact that there were signs of weakness on the horizon. Since then, the stock has tumbled by 17.7%. This is far worse than the 2.2% drop experienced by the S&P 500.

As disappointing as this is, the firm is still meaningfully outperforming the market since I originally rated it a "buy" way back in June of 2021. From that time through today, the S&P 500 is up a lofty 42.9%. But that pales in comparison to the 72.9% surge that shares of D.R. Horton experienced. Even though I fully expect weakness to continue in the near term, shares are still attractively priced on an absolute basis. Relative to similar companies, the stock is certainly on the pricier side of the spectrum. But even with this factored in, I would argue that the business is cheap enough to justify a "buy" rating.

Still a good home for investor money

In case you're not terribly familiar with D.R. Horton, the company operates as a massive homebuilder here in the US. According to management, the enterprise operates in 126 markets spread across 36 states. This includes major states like Pennsylvania, Florida, Texas, Georgia, and California. Since its inception back in 1978, the company has delivered over 1.1 million homes.

And the business claims that it operates as the largest builder in 62 different markets. This includes major areas like the Dallas-Fort Worth market where the company boasts an 18% market share, Houston where it has a 14% share of the market, Phoenix where it has a 12% share, San Antonio at 16%, Orlando at 16%, and Atlanta at 15%. The major market that it has the largest stake in, however, is Tampa. It has an astounding 24% market share there, though that still makes it the second-largest player in that area.

When scaling to this size, it's imperative to focus on as many customers as possible. Unlike some homebuilders, which focus more on higher end customers, D.R. Horton caters to more of a mass market buyer. 69% of the homes that it closed in the most recent quarter were priced at under $400,000. And only 12% of the homes closed were worth more than $500,000.

The most recent data that we have from management covers the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue came in at $7.73 billion. Even though this is an astounding amount of money, it's actually down 15.1% compared to the $9.11 billion reported one year earlier. This drop in revenue was driven by two factors. The biggest one was a decline in the number of homes closed from 22,548 to 19,276. At the same time, the company did experience a decline in the average price of a home from $375,500 to $372,500. The fact of the matter is that there has been weakness in the housing market. Nationwide, the median sales price of a new home was $403,600 in March of this year. That's down from the $436,400 reported the previous year. High interest rates and a cost-of-living crisis that exists in this country have negatively affected the industry.

The drop in revenue had a deleterious effect unprofitability as well. Net income plummeted from $1.17 billion to $810.4 million. All other important profitability metrics for the company worsened as well. Operating cash flow, for instance, went from negative $316.7 million to negative $436.2 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $1.25 billion to $958.9 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company contracted from $1.51 billion to $1.08 billion.

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. The drop in revenue, profits, and most cash flow metrics was driven by a decline in the number of homes closed from 41,888 to 38,335. Over the same window of time, the average price of a home closed by the company dropped from $375,800 to $373,700.

Even though I'm bullish about the company, there are some other indicators that are troubling. Take backlog as an example. At the end of the most recent quarter, the firm had 14,164 homes in backlog. That's a decline of 20.8% compared to the 17,873 homes that the business reported the same time one year earlier. To make matters worse, the total value of backlog has dropped because of not only the decline in the number of homes, but also because of a drop in the average price of a home in backlog from $393,900 to $386,700.

In all likelihood, the backlog will continue to shrink in the near term. I say this because net new orders of homes continue to come in weak. In the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, the company reported net new orders of 22,437 homes. That's a drop from the 26,456 homes reported the previous year. This rather substantial year-over-year decline brought the total number of homes ordered in the first half of this year down to 40,274 compared to the 44,525 reported for the first half of 2024. On top of this, the average price of a home ordered declined from $380,400 to $372,500. Cancellation rates are also ticking up slightly. For the first half of this year and last year, the cancellation rate for the business was 17%. But from the second quarter of 2024 to the second quarter of 2025, it increased slightly from 15% to 16%.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, management expects weakness to persist. They anticipate total deliveries of between 85,000 and 87,000 homes. At the midpoint, that would be down 4.1% from the 89,690 homes that the company closed in 2024. Management also expects revenue for the year to be between $33.3 billion and $34.8 billion. Even at the high end, this would represent a drop from the $36.80 billion the company reported last year. Not much was provided in the way of detail for profitability. The only thing that we know is that management is anticipating more than $3 billion in operating cash flow. If we annualize the results that we have seen so far for the year, we would get net profits of $3.71 billion, adjusted operating cash flow of $4.32 billion, and EBITDA of $4.93 billion.

Using these estimates, and also using the historical results for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. Even on a forward basis, shares of the business are trading at levels that I would consider to be reasonably attractive. But relative to similar companies, the stock is undoubtedly on the pricier end of the spectrum. In the table below, I stacked it up against five such firms. On a price to earnings basis, D.R. Horton ended up being the most expensive of the group. Four of the five companies ended up being cheaper than it is when using the EV to EBITDA approach. In fact, only on a price to operating cash flow basis is the business cheap relative to comparable enterprises. It ended up being the cheapest of the group using this approach.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
D.R. Horton 10.3 8.9 8.7
Taylor Morrison Home Corporation (TMHC) 6.8 14.7 6.0
Beazer Homes USA (BZH) 7.1 13.9 7.2
Toll Brothers (TOL) 8.1 11.6 7.2
Lennar (LEN) 8.0 16.9 6.5
PulteGroup (PHM) 7.2 13.4 5.4

Takeaway

To be honest with you, I think that difficult economic times are here again. I wouldn't be surprised if this makes things difficult on the homebuilding market for the next year or two. If tariffs are ultimately rescinded, I think the worst of the downturn could be avoided. But that doesn't seem terribly likely at the moment. For those who can't handle the volatility, it might be better to look elsewhere for opportunities. However, those focused on the long haul should view D.R. Horton in a favorable light. Management is optimistic enough that, in the first quarter of 2025, the company repurchased $1.3 billion worth of shares and paid out $125.5 million in the form of dividends. For the year in its entirety, the business is expected to buy back $4 billion worth of shares thanks to its new $5 billion share buyback program. This is all on top of the $500 million of dividends that management is forecasting. Given how cheap the stock is on an absolute basis, I would say this is a positive thing and it reinforces the ‘buy’ rating that I have on the business.
